content.title: Linear Combination
content.content:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\FF$ and let $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in V$. 
A **linear combination** of $v_1, \ldots, v_n$ 
is any vector of the form
\[
\alpha_1 v_1 + \alpha_2 v_2 + \cdots + \alpha_n v_n
\]
where $\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n \in \FF$. The scalars $\alpha_i$ are 
called the **coefficients** of the linear combination.

content.title: Span
content.content:
The **span** of a set $S = \{v_1, \ldots, v_n\} \subseteq V$ is 
the set of all linear combinations of vectors in $S$:
\[
\spn(S) = \spn\{v_1, \ldots, v_n\} = \{\alpha_1 v_1 + \cdots + \alpha_n v_n : \alpha_i \in \FF\}
\]
By convention, $\spn(\emptyset) = \{0\}$.

We say that $S$ **spans** $V$ (or that $S$ is a **spanning set** 
for $V$) if $\spn(S) = V$.

content.title: Span is a Subspace
content.content:
For any subset $S$ of a vector space $V$, $\spn(S)$ is a subspace of $V$. 
Moreover, $\spn(S)$ is the smallest subspace of $V$ containing $S$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We verify the subspace criteria. The zero vector is in $\spn(S)$ 
(take all coefficients equal to zero).

Let $u, w \in \spn(S)$, say $u = \sum \alpha_i v_i$ and $w = \sum \beta_i v_i$. 
For any $\gamma, \delta \in \FF$:
\[
\gamma u + \delta w = \sum (\gamma\alpha_i + \delta\beta_i) v_i \in \spn(S)
\]

For minimality: any subspace containing $S$ must contain all linear 
combinations of elements of $S$, hence must contain $\spn(S)$.

content.title: Standard Spanning Set
content.problem:
The vectors $e_1 = (1, 0, \ldots, 0)$, $e_2 = (0, 1, \ldots, 0)$, 
…, $e_n = (0, 0, \ldots, 1)$ span $\FF^n$:
\[
(a_1, \ldots, a_n) = a_1 e_1 + \cdots + a_n e_n
\]

content.title: Linear Independence
content.content:
A list $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ of vectors in $V$ is **linearly 
independent** if the only solution to
\[
\alpha_1 v_1 + \alpha_2 v_2 + \cdots + \alpha_n v_n = 0
\]
is $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \cdots = \alpha_n = 0$ (the **trivial solution**).

A list is **linearly dependent** if it 
is not linearly independent—that is, if there exist scalars 
$\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n$, not all zero, such that 
$\sum \alpha_i v_i = 0$.
The empty list is linearly independent by convention.

content.title: Characterization of Dependence
content.content:
A list $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ with $n \geq 1$ is linearly dependent if 
and only if some $v_j$ can be written as a linear combination of 
the preceding vectors $v_1, \ldots, v_{j-1}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
($\Leftarrow$) If $v_j = \sum_{i=1}^{j-1} \alpha_i v_i$, then
$\sum_{i=1}^{j-1} \alpha_i v_i - v_j + 0 \cdot v_{j+1} + \cdots = 0$
is a nontrivial linear combination equal to zero.

($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $\sum_{i=1}^n \alpha_i v_i = 0$ with not all 
$\alpha_i = 0$. Let $j$ be the largest index with $\alpha_j \neq 0$. Then
\[
\alpha_j v_j = -\sum_{i=1}^{j-1} \alpha_i v_i
\]
so $v_j = \sum_{i=1}^{j-1} (-\alpha_i/\alpha_j) v_i$.

content.title: Independence Inherited by Subsets
content.content:
If $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ is linearly independent, then so is any 
sublist $(v_{i_1}, \ldots, v_{i_k})$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $\sum_{s=1}^k \alpha_s v_{i_s} = 0$, extend to the full list by 
setting missing coefficients to zero. By independence of the full 
list, all coefficients are zero.

content.title: Dependence with Zero Vector
content.content: Any list containing the zero vector is linearly dependent.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $v_j = 0$, then $0 \cdot v_1 + \cdots + 1 \cdot v_j + \cdots + 0 \cdot v_n = 0$ 
is a nontrivial linear combination.

content.title: Basis
content.content:
A **basis** of a vector space $V$ is a list of 
vectors in $V$ that is both linearly independent and spanning.

content.title: Unique Representation
content.content:
A list $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ is a basis of $V$ if and only if every 
$v \in V$ can be written *uniquely* as
\[
v = \alpha_1 v_1 + \cdots + \alpha_n v_n
\]
for some $\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n \in \FF$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
($\Rightarrow$) Since $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ spans $V$, every $v$ has 
at least one representation. For uniqueness, suppose
\[
v = \alpha_1 v_1 + \cdots + \alpha_n v_n = \beta_1 v_1 + \cdots + \beta_n v_n
\]
Then $\sum (\alpha_i - \beta_i) v_i = 0$. By independence, 
$\alpha_i - \beta_i = 0$ for all $i$.

($\Leftarrow$) If every vector has at least one representation, the 
list spans. If every vector has at most one representation, applying 
this to $v = 0$ shows that $\sum \alpha_i v_i = 0$ implies all 
$\alpha_i = 0$, so the list is independent.

content.title: Coordinates
content.content:
If $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ is a basis of $V$ and $v = \sum \alpha_i v_i$, 
then the scalars $\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n$ are the 
**coordinates** of $v$ with respect to this basis.

content.title: Standard Basis
content.problem:
The **standard basis** of $\FF^n$ is 
$(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$ where $e_i$ has a 1 in position $i$ and 0 elsewhere. 
The coordinates of $(a_1, \ldots, a_n)$ are simply $a_1, \ldots, a_n$.

content.title: Polynomial Basis
content.problem: The list $(1, x, x^2, \ldots, x^n)$ is a basis of $\mathcal{P}_n(\FF)$.

content.text:
The following lemma is the key to proving that all bases have the 
same size.

content.title: Exchange Lemma (Steinitz)
content.content:
Let $V$ be a vector space spanned by a list $(w_1, \ldots, w_m)$. 
If $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ is linearly independent in $V$, then $n \leq m$.
content.label: Lemma

content.content:
We prove this by showing that we can successively replace $w$'s 
by $v$'s while maintaining a spanning list.

Since $(w_1, \ldots, w_m)$ spans $V$, we can write 
$v_1 = \sum_{j=1}^m \alpha_j w_j$. Since $v_1 \neq 0$ (as part of 
an independent list), some $\alpha_j \neq 0$. Reordering, assume 
$\alpha_1 \neq 0$. Then
\[
w_1 = \alpha_1^{-1} v_1 - \sum_{j=2}^m (\alpha_1^{-1}\alpha_j) w_j
\]
so $(v_1, w_2, \ldots, w_m)$ still spans $V$.

Now $v_2 = \beta_1 v_1 + \sum_{j=2}^m \beta_j w_j$. Since $v_2$ is 
not in $\spn\{v_1\}$ (by independence), some $\beta_j \neq 0$ for 
$j \geq 2$. Reorder so $\beta_2 \neq 0$, and solve for $w_2$. 
Then $(v_1, v_2, w_3, \ldots, w_m)$ spans.

Continue this process. After $k$ steps, $(v_1, \ldots, v_k, w_{k+1}, \ldots, w_m)$ 
spans. If $n > m$, we would run out of $w$'s before placing all $v$'s, 
but then $v_{m+1}$ would be in $\spn\{v_1, \ldots, v_m\}$, contradicting 
independence. Hence $n \leq m$.

content.title: All Bases Have the Same Size
content.content:
Any two bases of a finite-dimensional vector space have the same 
number of elements.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ and $(w_1, \ldots, w_m)$ be two bases of $V$. 
Applying the Exchange Lemma with the $v$'s as the independent list 
and the $w$'s as the spanning list gives $n \leq m$. Reversing roles 
gives $m \leq n$. Hence $m = n$.

content.title: Dimension
content.content:
The **dimension** of a finite-dimensional vector 
space $V$, denoted $\dim V$, is the number of vectors in any basis of $V$.

By convention, $\dim\{0\} = 0$.

content.title: Subspace Dimension
content.content:
If $W$ is a subspace of a finite-dimensional space $V$, then 
$\dim W \leq \dim V$.

Moreover, $\dim W = \dim V$ if and only if $W = V$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Any linearly independent list in $W$ is also linearly independent 
in $V$, so by the Exchange Lemma, its length is at most $\dim V$. 
Hence $\dim W \leq \dim V$.

If $\dim W = \dim V = n$, let $(w_1, \ldots, w_n)$ be a basis of $W$. 
This is an independent list of length $n$ in $V$, hence a basis of $V$ 
(by the theorem below). Thus $W = \spn\{w_1, \ldots, w_n\} = V$.

content.title: Basis Extension and Truncation
content.content:
Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional vector space with $\dim V = n$.

1. Any linearly independent list can be extended to a basis.

2. Any spanning list can be reduced to a basis.

3. Any linearly independent list of length $n$ is a basis.

4. Any spanning list of length $n$ is a basis.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(a) Let $(v_1, \ldots, v_k)$ be independent. If it spans, we're done. 
Otherwise, there exists $v_{k+1} \notin \spn\{v_1, \ldots, v_k\}$, 
and $(v_1, \ldots, v_{k+1})$ is independent. Continue until the 
list spans; this must happen by the time we have $n$ vectors.

(b) If $(w_1, \ldots, w_m)$ spans but is dependent, by 
Theorem [thm:dependence-characterization] some $w_j$ is a 
linear combination of earlier vectors. Remove it; the remaining 
list still spans. Continue until independent.

(c) By the Exchange Lemma, an independent list of length $n$ must 
span (otherwise we could extend it, getting an independent list 
of length $> n$, contradicting $\dim V = n$).

(d) If a spanning list of length $n$ were dependent, we could 
remove a vector and still span, obtaining a spanning list of 
length $< n$, contradicting $\dim V = n$.

content.title: Dimension of Sum
content.content:
If $U$ and $W$ are subspaces of a finite-dimensional space $V$, then
\[
\dim(U + W) = \dim U + \dim W - \dim(U \cap W)
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $(v_1, \ldots, v_k)$ be a basis of $U \cap W$. Extend to a 
basis $(v_1, \ldots, v_k, u_1, \ldots, u_r)$ of $U$ and to a 
basis $(v_1, \ldots, v_k, w_1, \ldots, w_s)$ of $W$.

We claim $(v_1, \ldots, v_k, u_1, \ldots, u_r, w_1, \ldots, w_s)$ 
is a basis of $U + W$.

*Spanning:* Any $x \in U + W$ can be written $x = u + w$ 
with $u \in U$, $w \in W$. Express $u$ and $w$ in their respective 
bases; this gives $x$ as a linear combination of the claimed basis.

*Independence:* Suppose $\sum \alpha_i v_i + \sum \beta_j u_j + \sum \gamma_\ell w_\ell = 0$. 
Then $\sum \gamma_\ell w_\ell = -\sum \alpha_i v_i - \sum \beta_j u_j \in U$. 
But also $\sum \gamma_\ell w_\ell \in W$, so $\sum \gamma_\ell w_\ell \in U \cap W$. 
Thus $\sum \gamma_\ell w_\ell = \sum \delta_i v_i$ for some scalars $\delta_i$, 
giving $\sum \gamma_\ell w_\ell - \sum \delta_i v_i = 0$. By independence 
of the basis of $W$, all $\gamma_\ell = 0$. Then 
$\sum \alpha_i v_i + \sum \beta_j u_j = 0$, so by independence of 
the basis of $U$, all $\alpha_i = 0$ and $\beta_j = 0$.

Therefore $\dim(U + W) = k + r + s = (k + r) + (k + s) - k = \dim U + \dim W - \dim(U \cap W)$.

content.title: Dimension of Direct Sum
content.content: If $V = U \oplus W$, then $\dim V = \dim U + \dim W$.
content.label: Corollary
